news_summary: Today’s technology news highlights groundbreaking advancements in ultra-fast imaging, innovative light-based nanotechnology, and deeper understanding of catalytic processes, alongside efforts to address the AI digital divide and promote global goodwill.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires dans le domaine de l'imagerie ultra-rapide, des nanotechnologies innovantes basées sur la lumière et une meilleure compréhension des processus catalytiques, ainsi que les efforts visant à réduire la fracture numérique liée à l'IA et à promouvoir la bonne volonté à l'échelle mondiale.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en el campo de la imagenología ultrarrápida, la nanotecnología innovadora basada en la luz y una comprensión más profunda de los procesos catalíticos, además de iniciativas para abordar la brecha digital en materia de inteligencia artificial y promover la buena voluntad a nivel mundial.

articles.title: This new camera captures what happens in a trillionth of a second
articles.summary: Scientists have unveiled a breakthrough imaging method that can capture the hidden details of events unfolding in trillionths of a second. This new technique doesn’t just track how bright something is—it also reveals subtle structural changes that were previously invisible, all in a single shot. By effectively turning ultrafast phenomena into detailed “movies,” researchers can now watch plasma form, electrons move, and materials transform in real time.
articles.content:
Researchers have created a powerful new imaging method that reveals far more detail about ultrafast events in the microscopic world than ever before. These processes unfold in incredibly short times, often within hundreds of femtoseconds, and have traditionally been difficult to study. The new approach allows scientists to observe and analyze these rapid changes with exceptional clarity and speed.

"In the fields of physics, chemistry, biology and materials science, many important phenomena happen incredibly fast," said research team leader Yunhua Yao from East China Normal University. "Our new technique can capture the complete evolution of both the brightness and internal structure of an object in a single measurement. This is a big step forward for understanding the fundamental nature of matter, designing new materials and even uncovering the mysteries of biological processes."

The team described their method in Optica, Optica Publishing Group's journal for high-impact research. The technique is known as compressed spectral-temporal coherent modulation femtosecond imaging (CST-CMFI). Using this system, the researchers were able to track ultrafast activity such as plasma forming in water after a femtosecond laser pulse and the behavior of excited charge carriers in ZnSe.

"Beyond helping scientists study materials that change instantly in response to laser light, chemical reactions that rearrange atoms at lightning speed and the dynamic behavior of biomolecules over incredibly short timescales, CST-CMFI could help improve high-power laser technologies used for clean energy research, advanced manufacturing and scientific instrumentation," said Yao. "It might also lead to the development of more efficient electronics, improved solar cells and faster devices by enabling a better understanding of how materials behave at extremely fast timescales."

Capturing More Than Brightness in Ultrafast Imaging

This work is part of ongoing efforts at the Extreme Optical Imaging Laboratory at East China Normal University to advance ultrafast camera technologies. A key focus is single-shot ultrafast optical imaging, which captures events that cannot be repeated by recording everything in a single exposure, similar to snapping a single frame that contains an entire sequence.

In the past, these techniques mainly recorded changes in brightness, also known as light intensity. However, light also carries phase information, which reveals how it bends or changes speed as it passes through materials. The researchers set out to capture both intensity and phase at the same time, providing a more complete picture of ultrafast processes.

To achieve this, they combined time-spectrum mapping, compressive spectral imaging and coherent modulation imaging. Each method contributes a specific advantage, including the ability to follow extremely fast changes, gather more data in one measurement and preserve fine image details.

How the CST-CMFI Technique Works

The system uses a chirped laser pulse made up of multiple wavelengths that arrive at slightly different times. This setup effectively links time to wavelength. When the pulse interacts with a fast-changing event, the scattered light carries detailed spatial, spectral and phase information. This information is then compressed into a single image through dispersion-encoded coherent modulation imaging.

A physics-informed neural network processes this data by separating the wavelengths and reconstructing both intensity and phase over time. Since each wavelength represents a specific moment, the result is a sequence of frames that forms an ultrafast movie captured in a single shot.

Real-Time Views of Plasma and Electron Behavior

To test the technique, the researchers examined two types of ultrafast phenomena. One experiment focused on plasma created in water by a femtosecond laser. Understanding how this plasma forms and evolves could support applications such as laser-based medical procedures. The imaging results revealed both brightness and phase changes within the plasma channel, including the formation of a dense free-electron plasma that affects how light is absorbed and how it travels through water.

The team also studied carrier dynamics in ZnSe to better understand how electrical charges move after being excited by light. Insights like these are important for improving optical and electronic devices made from this material, potentially leading to faster and more efficient technologies.

"Using CST-CMFI, we were able to see phase variations associated with the carrier dynamics, even when there were no significant changes in intensity," said Yao. "This highlights a key advantage of our method: Phase measurements can be much more sensitive than intensity measurements in detecting subtle ultrafast processes."

Expanding Applications and Future Improvements

Looking ahead, the researchers plan to apply the method to study additional phenomena, including interface dynamics and ultrafast phase transitions. These areas require detecting extremely small changes in the phase of light, making the new technique especially valuable.

At present, CST-CMFI converts spectral information into temporal information, which limits its ability to study processes that are highly sensitive to spectral changes. To address this, the team aims to combine CST-CMFI with compressive ultrafast photography. This next step would allow spectral and temporal information to be captured separately, significantly expanding the range of applications and improving the overall versatility of the technology.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in ultrafast imaging technology that enables scientists to capture detailed real-time movies of microscopic phenomena occurring in trillionths of a second. This advancement has broad implications across physics, chemistry, biology, and materials science, with potential applications in clean energy, advanced manufacturing, electronics, and medical procedures. The article provides detailed context about the method, its advantages, and future potential, demonstrating a meaningful positive impact on scientific understanding and technology development.
articles.personality_title: New camera captures events in a trillionth of a second with detailed clarity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long struggled to study extremely fast events that happen in tiny fractions of a second. These ultrafast processes, such as how electrons move or how materials change, happen so quickly they were hard to capture clearly.

**What happened** – A research team from East China Normal University developed a new camera method called CST-CMFI that can take a detailed “movie” of events happening in a trillionth of a second. This system records not just how bright something is, but also subtle changes in the structure of the object—all in a single shot. They tested it by watching plasma form in water and how electrical charges move in a material called ZnSe.

**Impact** – This technique is unique because it captures both brightness and phase information of light, giving a fuller picture of ultrafast events. It helps scientists see details they couldn’t before, which could improve technologies like solar cells, electronics, and even medical lasers. For example, understanding plasma in water better could enhance laser treatments.

**What’s next step** – The team plans to study other fast changes in materials and combine their method with another imaging technique to capture even more information. This will help scientists explore a wider range of fast processes and improve how they understand the microscopic world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new camera method lets scientists capture and study ultrafast microscopic events in unprecedented detail, opening doors for advances in technology and science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle caméra capture des événements en une trillionième de seconde avec une grande précision
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps eu du mal à étudier des événements extrêmement rapides qui se produisent en une fraction infime de seconde. Ces processus ultrarapides, comme le mouvement des électrons ou les changements dans les matériaux, sont si rapides qu’ils étaient difficiles à observer clairement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de recherche de l’Université Normale de Chine de l’Est a développé une nouvelle méthode d’imagerie appelée CST-CMFI qui peut filmer en détail des événements qui se produisent en une trillionième de seconde. Ce système enregistre non seulement la luminosité, mais aussi les changements subtils dans la structure de l’objet, le tout en une seule prise. Ils l’ont testé en observant la formation de plasma dans l’eau et le mouvement des charges électriques dans un matériau nommé ZnSe.

**Impact** – Cette technique est unique car elle capture à la fois la luminosité et la phase de la lumière, offrant une image plus complète des événements ultrarapides. Elle aide les scientifiques à voir des détails auparavant invisibles, ce qui pourrait améliorer des technologies comme les cellules solaires, l’électronique et même les lasers médicaux. Par exemple, mieux comprendre le plasma dans l’eau pourrait améliorer les traitements au laser.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’étudier d’autres changements rapides dans les matériaux et de combiner leur méthode avec une autre technique d’imagerie pour obtenir encore plus d’informations. Cela aidera les scientifiques à explorer un plus large éventail de processus rapides et à mieux comprendre le monde microscopique.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode de caméra permet aux scientifiques de capturer et d’étudier des événements microscopiques ultrarapides avec un détail sans précédent, ouvrant la voie à des avancées technologiques et scientifiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva cámara captura eventos en una billonésima de segundo con gran detalle
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han tenido dificultades para estudiar eventos extremadamente rápidos que ocurren en fracciones muy pequeñas de segundo. Estos procesos ultrarrápidos, como el movimiento de electrones o los cambios en materiales, suceden tan rápido que eran difíciles de observar claramente.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigación de la Universidad Normal del Este de China desarrolló un nuevo método de cámara llamado CST-CMFI que puede grabar una “película” detallada de eventos que ocurren en una billonésima de segundo. Este sistema captura no solo cuán brillante es algo, sino también cambios sutiles en la estructura del objeto, todo en una sola toma. Lo probaron observando cómo se forma plasma en el agua y cómo se mueven las cargas eléctricas en un material llamado ZnSe.

**Impacto** – Esta técnica es única porque captura tanto la intensidad como la fase de la luz, ofreciendo una imagen más completa de los eventos ultrarrápidos. Ayuda a los científicos a ver detalles que antes no podían, lo que podría mejorar tecnologías como células solares, electrónica e incluso láseres médicos. Por ejemplo, entender mejor el plasma en el agua podría mejorar los tratamientos con láser.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea estudiar otros cambios rápidos en materiales y combinar su método con otra técnica de imagen para capturar aún más información. Esto ayudará a los científicos a explorar una gama más amplia de procesos rápidos y a mejorar la comprensión del mundo microscópico.

**Mensaje clave en una frase** – Un nuevo método de cámara permite a los científicos capturar y estudiar eventos microscópicos ultrarrápidos con un detalle sin precedentes, abriendo puertas a avances en tecnología y ciencia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a translucent, glowing wave of light pulses flowing through a stylized microscopic water droplet, revealing swirling bright plasma streams and subtle, layered ripples of phase shifts around it, with delicate, spectral color gradients symbolizing ultrafast time and wavelength changes captured in a single frame.

articles.title: Scientists sculpt Einstein onto a crystal using only light
articles.summary: A light-sensitive crystal is opening the door to a new era of “light-written” technology. Arsenic trisulfide can be reshaped and permanently altered using simple light, creating ultra-fine optical patterns without expensive manufacturing tools. Scientists even etched a nanoscale portrait of Einstein and high-density patterns that could act as secure optical signatures. This breakthrough could power everything from advanced sensors to next-generation AR devices.
articles.content:
Researchers from the XPANCEO Emerging Technologies Research Center, working with Nobel Laureate Prof. Konstantin Novoselov (University of Manchester and the National University of Singapore), have uncovered unusual optical behavior in arsenic trisulfide (As 2 S 3 ), a crystalline van der Waals semiconductor. Their findings show that this material can be permanently altered by light and even shaped at the nanoscale using simple continuous-wave (CW) light. This approach avoids the need for costly cleanroom fabrication or advanced femtosecond laser systems.

A key concept behind this discovery is the refractive index, which describes how much a material bends or slows light. Materials with higher refractive indices are better at confining and directing light within devices. In certain materials, light can also change this property. This effect, known as photorefractivity, occurs when exposure to light alters the refractive index.

In crystalline As 2 S 3 , this response happens even under low-intensity ultraviolet light. The study reports an exceptionally large change in refractive index (up to Δn ≈ 0.3), which exceeds the values typically observed in well-known photorefractive materials such as BaTiO 3 or LiNbO 3 .

Why Strong Photorefractivity Matters for Technology

Materials that respond strongly to light in this way are highly useful because they allow optical functions to be directly written into the material. Instead of relying on multiple mechanical or manufacturing steps, light itself can define how a device handles and directs light.

This capability is important for many everyday technologies. It supports the creation of tiny structures that guide signals in telecommunications systems, enables compact optical components used in sensors and imaging devices, and allows the formation of hologram-like features used in product authentication and security.

Nanoscale Optical Patterns and "Optical Fingerprints"

In As 2 S 3 , the effect is especially powerful at very small scales. The large change in refractive index allows the formation of extremely fine patterns that remain embedded in the transparent material. These patterns act as unique optical identifiers that are difficult to replicate, making them useful for anti-counterfeiting and traceability applications.

To demonstrate this precision, the researchers used a standard laser to create a microscopic monochrome portrait of Albert Einstein on a thin piece of the material, with points spaced just 700 nanometers apart. Further experiments showed that the technique can achieve even finer resolution (to ~50,000 dots per inch, which corresponds to 500 nanometers between points). The resulting patterns show strong optical contrast because of the light-induced refractive index changes, making them easy to detect with optical methods.

Light-Driven Materials and the Future of Photonics

"The discovery of new functional materials, particularly within the unique family of van der Waals crystals, is the fundamental engine for moving the entire field of photonics forward. Developing sophisticated optical devices, such as advanced smart contact lenses, is a deeply complex challenge that requires a solid foundation in fundamental materials science. In these systems, the material itself is the key component that determines what is physically possible. By identifying natural crystals with this level of sensitivity, we are effectively providing the essential building blocks for a new generation of technology that is driven entirely by light rather than electricity," said Valentyn Volkov, Founder and Chief Technology Officer at the XPANCEO Emerging Technologies Research Center.

Expanding Crystals Enable New Optical Devices

Beyond patterning, As 2 S 3 also changes physically when exposed to light. The material can expand by as much as 5%, allowing researchers to directly form optical structures such as microlenses and diffraction gratings on its surface. These capabilities are important for building wide field-of-view waveguides used in augmented reality glasses and smart contact lenses.

The material's responsiveness also makes it promising for use in photonic circuits and nanoscale sensors. Together, these properties represent a significant step forward in controlling and manipulating light for next-generation technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough where a light-sensitive crystal (arsenic trisulfide) can be permanently reshaped using simple light to create nanoscale optical patterns. This discovery enables new, cost-effective manufacturing methods for advanced optical devices with broad applications in sensors, telecommunications, augmented reality, and security. The impact is broad and meaningful for future technology development, with detailed context and implications provided.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use light to sculpt tiny portraits and patterns on crystals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are exploring new materials that can be changed by light to make tiny shapes and patterns. This helps create advanced optical devices without expensive tools.

**What happened** – Researchers from XPANCEO Emerging Technologies and Nobel Laureate Prof. Konstantin Novoselov discovered that arsenic trisulfide (As2S3), a crystal, can be permanently reshaped using simple continuous light. They used this crystal to etch a tiny portrait of Albert Einstein with points only 700 nanometers apart, showing how finely the material can be controlled.

**Impact** – This crystal changes how it bends light when exposed to ultraviolet light in a very strong way. This lets scientists write optical patterns directly into the material, creating unique “optical fingerprints.” These can be used for secure product identification, sensors, and devices like smart glasses or augmented reality lenses. The ability to shape the crystal with light instead of machines makes the process cheaper and more precise.

**What's next step** – Researchers will explore using this crystal to build new optical devices such as tiny lenses and circuits for future technologies. This could lead to better sensors, improved telecommunications, and advanced displays that rely on light rather than electricity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a way to use light to create tiny, permanent patterns on crystals, opening new possibilities for affordable and precise optical technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques sculptent de minuscules portraits sur des cristaux grâce à la lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques étudient de nouveaux matériaux qui peuvent être modifiés par la lumière pour créer de minuscules formes et motifs. Cela permet de fabriquer des appareils optiques avancés sans outils coûteux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du centre XPANCEO et le lauréat du prix Nobel, le professeur Konstantin Novoselov, ont découvert que le trisulfure d'arsenic (As2S3), un cristal, peut être modifié de façon permanente avec une lumière continue simple. Ils ont gravé un petit portrait d'Albert Einstein avec des points espacés de seulement 700 nanomètres, montrant la précision possible.

**Impact** – Ce cristal change fortement la façon dont il plie la lumière sous lumière ultraviolette. Cela permet d’écrire directement des motifs optiques dans le matériau, créant des « empreintes optiques » uniques. Ces motifs peuvent servir pour l’identification sécurisée de produits, des capteurs, et des dispositifs comme des lunettes intelligentes ou des lentilles de réalité augmentée. Façonner le cristal avec la lumière rend le procédé moins cher et plus précis.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont utiliser ce cristal pour fabriquer de nouveaux dispositifs optiques, comme des lentilles et circuits minuscules pour des technologies futures. Cela pourrait améliorer les capteurs, les télécommunications et les écrans utilisant la lumière plutôt que l’électricité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé comment utiliser la lumière pour créer des motifs minuscules et permanents sur des cristaux, ouvrant la voie à des technologies optiques précises et abordables.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos esculpen diminutos retratos en cristales usando luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos están investigando nuevos materiales que pueden cambiarse con luz para crear formas y patrones muy pequeños. Esto ayuda a fabricar dispositivos ópticos avanzados sin herramientas caras.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del centro XPANCEO y el premio Nobel Konstantin Novoselov descubrieron que el trisulfuro de arsénico (As2S3), un cristal, puede cambiarse permanentemente usando luz continua simple. Usaron este cristal para grabar un pequeño retrato de Albert Einstein con puntos separados por solo 700 nanómetros, demostrando un control muy fino.

**Impacto** – Este cristal cambia mucho la forma en que dobla la luz bajo luz ultravioleta. Esto permite escribir patrones ópticos directamente en el material, creando “huellas ópticas” únicas. Estos patrones pueden usarse para identificación segura de productos, sensores y dispositivos como lentes inteligentes o de realidad aumentada. Moldear el cristal con luz hace que el proceso sea más barato y preciso.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores explorarán usar este cristal para fabricar nuevos dispositivos ópticos como lentes y circuitos diminutos para tecnologías futuras. Esto podría mejorar sensores, telecomunicaciones y pantallas que usan luz en lugar de electricidad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han descubierto cómo usar luz para crear patrones diminutos y permanentes en cristales, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para tecnologías ópticas precisas y económicas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a translucent crystal block softly glowing under gentle ultraviolet light, with a delicate, intricate nanoscale portrait of Einstein subtly etched inside the crystal’s surface, surrounded by fine luminous light patterns symbolizing precise light-driven refractive index changes and tiny optical structures forming like expanding ripples within the crystal.

articles.title: Breakthrough discovery reveals hidden oxygen flow deep inside catalysts
articles.summary: A major discovery is reshaping how scientists think about catalysts. Researchers have, for the first time, captured oxygen atoms moving through the interior of a catalyst—not just along its surface. This reveals that the bulk material can actively participate in reactions, opening a new frontier in catalyst design. The finding could lead to smarter, more efficient systems by harnessing this hidden internal pathway.
articles.content:
A team led by Prof. Tao Zhang and Prof. Yanqiang Huang at the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics (DICP) of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), working with Prof. Wei Liu of DICP and Prof. Yanggang Wang of the Southern University of Science and Technology, has directly tracked oxygen movement in catalysts. Using environmental transmission electron microscopy, they observed bulk oxygen spillover in Ru/rutile-TiO2 catalysts for the first time. The discovery points to new ways of using the interior of catalysts, which has often been overlooked.

The findings were published in Nature on April 15, 2026.

What Is Oxygen Spillover in Catalysis

In catalytic reactions, spillover refers to the movement of atoms or molecules such as hydrogen or oxygen between a metal and the material that supports it. Most past research has focused on spillover occurring along the surface of catalysts. It has remained uncertain whether the interior, or bulk, of a catalyst also plays a role in these processes through non-surface pathways.

Understanding spillover is important because it influences how different active sites interact. It can change how many of these sites are available and affect how well a catalyst performs. Previous work has shown that materials capable of being reduced can improve spillover on surfaces, depending on how far and how fast atoms move. However, traditional spectroscopic techniques have struggled to reveal the exact pathways involved at the level of individual particles. Gaining a clearer picture could help scientists better control reactions that depend on spillover.

Why Titanium Dioxide Was Chosen

The researchers selected titanium dioxide (TiO 2 ) because it can store and release oxygen efficiently. Its ability to change oxidation states, along with its variety of crystal structures, makes it a useful model for studying oxygen behavior. Using environmental transmission electron microscopy, the team was able to directly observe oxygen movement on individual ruthenium on titanium dioxide (Ru/TiO 2 ) particles.

First Direct Evidence of Bulk Oxygen Spillover

For decades, scientists believed that spillover mainly took place on catalyst surfaces. In this study, the team provided the first direct observation of oxygen moving within the bulk of a catalyst in ruthenium supported on rutile titanium dioxide (Ru/r-TiO 2 ).

"A channel has been disclosed in TiO 2 support to facilitate oxygen spillover, meanwhile the metal-support interface acts like an atomic scale guard, controlling whether oxygen spillover can pass through. This finding inspires a new strategy for utilizing catalyst bulk that is conventionally believed useless in catalysis," said Prof. Wei Liu.

Oxygen Movement Beneath the Surface

The researchers showed that oxygen atoms travel through the (Ru/r-TiO 2 ) interface from layers located three to five atoms below the surface of r-TiO 2 to the metal. This movement is driven by differences in oxygen chemical potential.

"This unique oxygen spillover in our work enables the bulk of a catalyst, which is otherwise inaccessible to reactants, to contribute to mass transfer during catalytic reactions, underscoring the critical importance of interface engineering in controlling spillover behavior," said Prof. Yanqiang Huang.

Expanding the Concept of Metal-Support Interaction

Nearly 50 years ago, scientists identified metal-support interactions, where metal particles become surrounded by oxide materials such as TiO 2 under strongly reducing conditions. This process can reduce the ability of the metal to adsorb molecules like H 2 and CO. Traditionally, these interactions were thought to involve material exchange only at the outer surfaces of metals and their supports, with the boundary between them playing a key role in reactions.

This new work expands that concept by showing that bulk oxygen spillover allows the inner regions of a catalyst to take part in mass transfer during reactions. These internal interfaces were previously considered inaccessible.

Toward More Efficient Catalyst Design

The findings highlight how important interface engineering is for controlling spillover behavior. They also demonstrate the power of in situ microscopic imaging at the single-particle level for uncovering reaction pathways in catalytic systems.

Looking ahead, the researchers aim to build on this discovery. "Taking this excellent opportunity, we can improve architecture of catalysis from two-dimensional surface reactions to the three dimensional 'surface-interface-bulk' synergy. It provides fresh insights into interfacial atomic engineering in heterogeneous catalysis and the dynamic catalytic behavior of supported metal catalyst. The next goal is to develop practical catalysts that utilize the bulk to directly contribute to chemical reactions," said Prof. Tao Zhang.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in catalysis by discovering oxygen flow inside catalyst bulk, which reshapes understanding and opens new pathways for designing more efficient catalysts. This has broad implications for chemical industries and environmental technologies, offering potential large-scale benefits. The article provides detailed context, significance, and future outlook, meeting the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe oxygen moving inside catalysts for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Catalysts help speed up chemical reactions and are used in many industries. Until now, scientists believed oxygen atoms moved only on the surface of catalysts during these reactions.

**What happened** – A team of researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Southern University of Science and Technology used a special microscope to watch oxygen atoms moving inside a catalyst made of ruthenium and titanium dioxide. This is the first time oxygen movement inside the bulk, or inner part, of a catalyst has been directly seen.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that the inside of catalysts can also take part in chemical reactions, not just the surface. It changes how scientists understand catalysts and could lead to designing smarter and more efficient catalysts by using both the surface and the inside for reactions.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to create new catalysts that use this internal oxygen movement to improve performance. They plan to study how to design catalysts with better control of these inside pathways to help chemical processes work faster and cleaner.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that oxygen atoms can travel inside catalysts, opening new ways to make chemical reactions faster and more efficient.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques observent pour la première fois le mouvement de l’oxygène à l’intérieur des catalyseurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les catalyseurs accélèrent les réactions chimiques et sont utilisés dans de nombreuses industries. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques pensaient que les atomes d’oxygène ne se déplaçaient que sur la surface des catalyseurs pendant ces réactions.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs de l’Académie chinoise des sciences et de l’Université des sciences et technologies du Sud a utilisé un microscope spécial pour observer les atomes d’oxygène se déplaçant à l’intérieur d’un catalyseur composé de ruthénium et de dioxyde de titane. C’est la première fois que ce mouvement à l’intérieur, ou dans la partie interne, d’un catalyseur est vu directement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que l’intérieur des catalyseurs peut aussi participer aux réactions chimiques, pas seulement la surface. Cela change la compréhension des catalyseurs et pourrait conduire à concevoir des catalyseurs plus intelligents et efficaces en utilisant à la fois la surface et l’intérieur.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent créer de nouveaux catalyseurs qui utilisent ce mouvement interne de l’oxygène pour améliorer leurs performances. Ils prévoient d’étudier comment concevoir des catalyseurs avec un meilleur contrôle de ces chemins internes pour rendre les processus chimiques plus rapides et plus propres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert que les atomes d’oxygène peuvent circuler à l’intérieur des catalyseurs, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour accélérer et améliorer les réactions chimiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan por primera vez el movimiento de oxígeno dentro de catalizadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los catalizadores ayudan a acelerar las reacciones químicas y se usan en muchas industrias. Hasta ahora, los científicos pensaban que los átomos de oxígeno solo se movían en la superficie de los catalizadores durante estas reacciones.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores de la Academia China de Ciencias y la Universidad de Ciencia y Tecnología del Sur usó un microscopio especial para observar átomos de oxígeno moviéndose dentro de un catalizador hecho de rutenio y dióxido de titanio. Es la primera vez que se observa directamente el movimiento de oxígeno dentro de la parte interna de un catalizador.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el interior de los catalizadores también puede participar en las reacciones químicas, no solo la superficie. Cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden los catalizadores y podría llevar a diseñar catalizadores más inteligentes y eficientes usando tanto la superficie como el interior.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren crear nuevos catalizadores que usen este movimiento interno de oxígeno para mejorar su desempeño. Planean estudiar cómo diseñar catalizadores con mejor control de estas rutas internas para que los procesos químicos sean más rápidos y limpios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han descubierto que los átomos de oxígeno pueden moverse dentro de los catalizadores, abriendo nuevas formas de acelerar y mejorar las reacciones químicas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned scientific illustration depicting a cross-section of a catalyst particle where glowing oxygen atoms flow like luminous streams beneath the surface layers of a textured titanium dioxide crystal lattice, seamlessly bridging to a cluster of stylized ruthenium metal atoms at the interface, symbolizing the hidden internal oxygen spillover and dynamic interaction between metal and support within the catalyst’s bulk.

articles.title: World News in Brief: Tackling the AI digital divide, deadly migration journeys, Lucy Hale named new WFP Goodwill Ambassador
articles.summary: UN-led efforts are continuing to ensure that all countries can benefit and regulate Artificial Intelligence (AI) as it increasingly shapes our economies, societies and daily lives.
articles.content:
The UN’s Global Dialogue on Artificial Intelligence Governance is one of the key initiatives on AI.

It brings together all 193 United Nations Member States, the private sector, civil society, academia and the tech world to share best practices and build common approaches to AI governance.

The Dialogue’s first meeting takes place in Geneva in July; its discussions will draw on data that’s being put together by another UN AI initiative – the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI.

That’s being headed by campaigning Philippines journalist Maria Ressa and iconic computer scientist, Yoshua Bengio.

‘Science and evidence based’

UN Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies, Amandeep Gill, said that the debate over policy would be “science and evidence-based,” with a variety of perspectives considered.

“This is how policy discussions should be, and the UN is very proud to facilitate this first ever such confluence of science and policy in a fast-paced emerging technology.”

Ahead of the UN’s AI governance meeting in Switzerland, discussions are continuing between all the parties involved to ensure that the best national initiatives on how to best use the technology can be shared.

Priorities for the Swiss meeting include promoting respect for human rights, drawing up safety measures to prevent the technology from causing harm, and discussing measures to bridge a huge AI access gap, explained co-chairs of the Dialogue, Egriselda López and Rein Tammsaar.

In responses to media questions, the co-chairs and Mr. Gill clarified that military uses of AI would not be part of the discussion but they will address general safety and human rights concerns.

They underscored optimism that the UN can serve as a global hub for sharing best practices and advancing an action-oriented, inclusive approach to AI governance.

Tweet URL

Shifting migration routes claiming more lives, UN agency warns

Nearly 8,000 migrants were reported dead or missing worldwide in 2025, bringing the total recorded fatalities since 2014 to over 82,000, the International Organization for Migration (IOM) revealed on Tuesday.

New data from IOM’s Missing Migrants Project and the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) shows that while arrivals have declined in some regions, migration routes are shifting rather than easing. Enforcement measures and conflict are pushing people toward longer, more fragmented, and increasingly dangerous journeys.

“Routes are shifting in response to conflict, climate pressures, and policy changes, but the risks are still very real,” said IOM Director General Amy Pope.

She emphasized that an estimated 340,000 family members have been directly affected by these disappearances.

Significant shift

The report highlights significant shifts: while northbound movements through Central America and arrivals to the Canary Islands dropped, journeys became more geographically dispersed. In Europe, Bangladeshi nationals emerged as the largest arrival group as Syrian numbers fell.

Ahead of May’s International Migration Review Forum, IOM is calling for stronger international cooperation.

The agency stresses that lower arrival figures do not equate to safer journeys, urging sustained investment in evidence-based responses to save lives and support affected families.

Actress Lucy Hale newest WFP Goodwill Ambassador

American actress and producer Lucy Hale – star of the hit television series Pretty Little Liars – has been named a Goodwill Ambassador with the World Food Programme (WFP), the UN agency announced on Tuesday.

Tweet URL

She will advocate on behalf of the more than 318 million people worldwide facing extreme hunger.

Ms. Hale is the daughter of a Tennessee farmer and a nurse. She has raised her voice on behalf of women and children and cares deeply about food security, small-scale farmers, and children’s health and education.

Honour, passion and commitment

“It is the honour of a lifetime to become a WFP Goodwill Ambassador,” she said. “It has long been my dream to give back in this way, and I am eager to shine a spotlight on WFP’s critical work to save and change lives around the world.”

Ms. Hale has been raising awareness and generating support for WFP’s work since 2024, including in Sudan, Gaza, Myanmar, and Ukraine.

“In a world where so many people go to bed hungry, we are fortunate to have someone as passionate and committed as Lucy partnering with us,” said WFP Executive Director Cindy McCain.

“Her voice will elevate attention to people and places too often overlooked – and help drive the action needed to reach them.”

Ms. Hale joins a roster of WFP Goodwill Ambassadors, including fellow Americans actress Kate Hudson and fashion designer Michael Kors, Tunisian tennis player Ons Jabeur, and Canadian singer and songwriter The Weeknd, whose real name is Abel Tesfaye.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant UN-led initiative to govern AI inclusively and safely, involving all member states and key stakeholders, aiming to bridge the AI access gap and promote human rights. This represents a meaningful, large-scale effort with broad societal impact and detailed context on the governance framework being developed.
articles.personality_title: UN launches global talks to guide safe and fair use of AI
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming a bigger part of everyday life and affects economies and societies worldwide. The United Nations is working to help all countries manage AI safely and fairly.

**What happened** – The UN has started the Global Dialogue on Artificial Intelligence Governance. This meeting, involving all 193 UN member countries plus experts from businesses, universities, and civil groups, will begin in Geneva in July. They will share ideas and create rules to guide how AI should be used. A scientific panel led by journalist Maria Ressa and scientist Yoshua Bengio is providing important data to support these talks.

**Impact** – This is the first time the UN is bringing together so many different groups to make AI policies based on science and real evidence. The talks will focus on protecting human rights, making AI safe, and closing the gap between countries that have easy access to AI and those that do not. Military uses of AI won’t be discussed, keeping the focus on general safety and fairness.

**What's next step** – The dialogue will continue with discussions before and after the Geneva meeting. Countries will share their best ideas and work together to create clear rules for AI. This effort aims to build a global system that helps everyone use AI responsibly and fairly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is leading a worldwide effort to create fair and safe rules for AI, involving all countries and experts to ensure the technology benefits everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance des discussions mondiales pour un usage sûr et équitable de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) joue un rôle de plus en plus important dans la vie quotidienne et influence les économies et les sociétés du monde entier. L’Organisation des Nations Unies travaille à aider tous les pays à gérer l’IA en toute sécurité et équité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’ONU a lancé le Dialogue mondial sur la gouvernance de l’intelligence artificielle. Cette réunion, qui réunit les 193 pays membres de l’ONU ainsi que des experts du secteur privé, du monde universitaire et de la société civile, débutera en juillet à Genève. Ils partageront des idées et établiront des règles pour guider l’utilisation de l’IA. Un panel scientifique dirigé par la journaliste Maria Ressa et le scientifique Yoshua Bengio fournit des données importantes pour soutenir ces discussions.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’ONU réunit autant d’acteurs différents pour créer des politiques sur l’IA basées sur la science et des preuves concrètes. Les discussions porteront sur la protection des droits humains, la sécurité de l’IA et la réduction de l’écart entre les pays qui ont un accès facile à l’IA et ceux qui n’en ont pas. Les usages militaires de l’IA ne seront pas abordés, pour se concentrer sur la sécurité et l’équité.

**Prochaine étape** – Le dialogue se poursuivra avant et après la réunion de Genève. Les pays partageront leurs meilleures idées et travailleront ensemble pour créer des règles claires pour l’IA. Cet effort vise à construire un système mondial qui aide tout le monde à utiliser l’IA de manière responsable et équitable.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’ONU mène un effort mondial pour créer des règles justes et sûres pour l’IA, impliquant tous les pays et experts afin que cette technologie profite à tous.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU inicia diálogos globales para un uso seguro y justo de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) está tomando un papel cada vez más importante en la vida diaria y afecta las economías y sociedades en todo el mundo. Las Naciones Unidas están trabajando para ayudar a todos los países a manejar la IA de forma segura y justa.

**Qué pasó** – La ONU ha iniciado el Diálogo Global sobre Gobernanza de la Inteligencia Artificial. Esta reunión, que incluye a los 193 países miembros de la ONU junto con expertos de empresas, universidades y grupos civiles, comenzará en Ginebra en julio. Compartirán ideas y crearán reglas para guiar el uso de la IA. Un panel científico liderado por la periodista Maria Ressa y el científico Yoshua Bengio está proporcionando datos importantes para apoyar estas conversaciones.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que la ONU reúne a tantos grupos diferentes para hacer políticas de IA basadas en ciencia y evidencia real. Las charlas se centrarán en proteger los derechos humanos, hacer la IA segura y cerrar la brecha entre países con acceso fácil a la IA y los que no lo tienen. No se discutirán usos militares de la IA, manteniendo el enfoque en la seguridad y la justicia.

**Próximo paso** – El diálogo continuará con discusiones antes y después de la reunión en Ginebra. Los países compartirán sus mejores ideas y trabajarán juntos para crear reglas claras para la IA. Este esfuerzo busca construir un sistema global que ayude a todos a usar la IA responsable y justamente.

**Resumen en una frase** – La ONU lidera un esfuerzo mundial para crear reglas justas y seguras para la IA, involucrando a todos los países y expertos para que la tecnología beneficie a todos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting showing a diverse group of silhouetted figures from around the world connected by glowing threads of light symbolizing AI governance and cooperation, alongside a gentle river winding through a fragmented landscape representing shifting migration routes, and a radiant wheat sheaf held aloft by abstract hands symbolizing food security and global humanitarian support.
